senses.definition: to earn enough money to be able to pay for the necessities of life

examples.x: As the sole breadwinner, John works hard to bring home the bacon for his family.
During the tough times, he was still bringing home the bacon to keep the family afloat.
If I get this promotion, I'll be able to bring home the bacon and take care of my family better.
Last year, she brought home the bacon by closing the big deal for her company.
Sarah is out there, bringing home the bacon by excelling in her career.

senses.definition: the main source of a person or business's income

examples.x: At the moment, freelancing is her bread and butter while she explores other career options.
Once she establishes her bakery, she hopes it will become her bread and butter for the rest of her life.
Teaching is his bread and butter; he has been an educator for over two decades.
When he was younger, acting was his bread and butter, and it provided for all his needs.

senses.definition: to manage to earn enough money to buy essential things in one's life

examples.x: As the project leader, I will make sure to keep the kettle boiling until we reach the desired outcome.
Despite the challenges, they managed to keep the pot boiling and achieved their goals.
She is working hard, keeping the kettle boiling for the research project until it is completed.
The team needs to keep the pot boiling and not lose focus on their objectives.

senses.definition: to be capable of providing for one's most essential needs

examples.x: After losing everything, she wonders how she will keep body and soul together in the coming months.
Despite the hardships, she's managing to keep body and soul together by working multiple jobs.
When he was unemployed, it was challenging for him to keep body and soul together, but he eventually found a job.
With the current economic situation, he's doing whatever job he can to keep body and soul together.

senses.definition: to make enough money to pay for one's basic needs

examples.x: During tough times, they had to cut back on expenses to make ends meet.
She is working extra hours to make both ends meet while she looks for a better-paying job.
With the new job offer, he hopes to make ends meet more comfortably in the coming months.
He has two jobs just to make ends meet each month.

senses.definition: to make enough money to afford the necessities of life

examples.x: During the recession, many families found it challenging to keep the wolf from the door.
If she gets the promotion, it will help her keep the wolves from the door.
She is working hard to keep the wolf from the door, but the high rent makes it difficult.
With a low-paying job, he struggles to keep the wolf from the door each month.

senses.definition: to make a lot of money in a fast way

examples.x: During the boom in the real estate market, he coined money by selling properties.
If the business expansion goes as planned, they will be coining money in the coming years.
The company's new product line is coining money and gaining popularity.
With her innovative business idea, she is coining money in the competitive market.

senses.definition: to earn a large amount of money, often in a short period of time and with little effort, usually through a successful business venture, investment, etc,

examples.x: By investing in tech stocks, he made a killing in the stock market this year.
Despite the competition, they were making a killing with their unique business model.
She made a killing by selling her startup to a larger company.
The company is making a killing with its latest product launch.
The investor made a killing on the cryptocurrency boom.

senses.definition: to earn a lot of money

examples.x: If the new business strategy works, they could make a mint in the coming years.
Last year, the company made a mint by securing a major contract.
The real estate market is booming, and developers are currently making a mint.
With his new invention, he is making a mint in the tech industry.

word_list.word: [laugh] all the way to the bank
word_list.pronunciation: lˈæf ˈɔːl ðə wˈeɪ tə ðə bˈæŋk

senses.definition: to gain significant wealth, often by doing something that is considered useless or foolish by many

examples.x: After winning the lottery, she is laughing all the way to the bank with her newfound wealth.
Last year, their blockbuster movie had them laughing all the way to the bank with record-breaking box office earnings.
With their successful startup, they are laughing all the way to the bank as profits continue to rise.

senses.definition: to gain money using methods that are illegal or dishonest

examples.x: He lined his pocket by scamming unsuspecting investors with a Ponzi scheme.
If they don't take appropriate measures, they might end up lining their pockets with illegal gains.
Some executives are accused of lining their pockets by embezzling company funds.
The corrupt politician was caught using his position to line his own pockets with bribes.

senses.definition: a commercial activity that enables its participants to become rich without making much effort

examples.x: During the tech boom, some startups had a license to print money with their innovative products.
If the investment works out as planned, it could be a license to print money for the investors.
The successful author's latest book is selling so well that it seems like a license to print money.
With the new product's popularity, the company seems to have a license to print money.

senses.definition: a highly desirable job or position, often characterized by excellent pay, benefits, working conditions, and opportunities for advancement

examples.x: After years of hard work, he finally secured a plum job as the head of the marketing department.
During the economic boom, many professionals were landing plum jobs with attractive benefits.
He was thrilled when he landed a plum job at a top-tier tech company.
If she gets the scholarship, it will help her pursue her dream of a plum job in the fashion industry.
She is pursuing her MBA to increase her chances of getting a plum job in the finance sector.

senses.definition: money that one can earn without making much effort

examples.x: By flipping vintage clothing, she is making money for old rope on a regular basis.
During the event, they made money for old rope by selling old memorabilia at high prices.
If they can secure the contract, it will be like money for old rope with guaranteed income.
Selling refurbished furniture online can be like money for old rope if you find the right market.

senses.definition: a type of ability where one can make a profit from anything that one does

examples.x: During his career, he had the Midas touch with various investments and accumulated significant wealth.
If he takes charge of the project, he might bring his Midas touch and turn it into a huge success.
She seems to have the Midas touch in the stock market, making smart investments consistently.
With his Midas touch, every business he starts becomes a thriving enterprise.

senses.definition: to participate in buying and selling stocks and other financial instruments in the hopes of making a profit

examples.x: He is an experienced investor who knows how to play the market effectively.
If he decides to play the market aggressively, he might incur higher risks but potentially higher rewards.
Last year, she played the market well and earned substantial returns on her investments.
Lately, many investors are playing the market cautiously due to uncertain economic conditions.

senses.definition: used to say that an amount of money is gained as profit in a transaction
a good pension or a large salary given to certain employees in order to encourage a longer employment term

examples.x: Despite being offered another job, he's reluctant to leave because of the golden handcuffs the current company provides.
She stayed with the company longer than she wanted to due to the allure of golden handcuffs.
The tech company is using golden handcuffs to retain its top talent amidst fierce competition.

senses.definition: a financial compensation that is offered to a top executive in the event that their employment is terminated

examples.x: As the company faces a potential merger, the executives are concerned about the future of their golden parachutes.
The CEO negotiated a golden parachute in her contract to ensure financial security if the company undergoes a takeover.
When the CEO resigned, he received a generous golden parachute as part of his exit package.

senses.definition: a payment or package given to a worker, usually a high-ranking manager, when they leave a company

examples.x: During the organizational changes, many employees were considering whether to accept the golden handshake offers.
If the company downsizes, there might be several employees eligible for golden handshakes.
The company is planning to restructure, and some senior employees may be offered golden handshakes to encourage early retirement.
The retiring CEO received a lavish golden handshake as a token of appreciation for his long service to the company.
When the senior executive left the firm, she was granted a generous golden handshake as part of her departure agreement.

senses.definition: used of a person who is unemployed but receives regular payment or benefits from the government

examples.x: After losing his job, he has been on the dole for several months while searching for new employment.
Due to the economic downturn, many people are finding themselves on the dole for extended periods.
During the recession, more and more people were ending up on the dole as businesses struggled.
If the company downsizes, many employees could find themselves on the dole.
When the factory closed down, hundreds of workers were suddenly on the dole.
